context: Current closing price of Halliburton (HAL): $39.11 USD as of 2026-04-22.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift across time horizons, starting near 39.3 at the one-day mark and gradually rising to 40.0 at one month. This pattern suggests I was anchoring heavily on the most recent observed value and applying a slight mean-reversion or trend assumption — essentially expecting the series to drift slowly toward some longer-run equilibrium slightly above current levels. At the one-day horizon, the estimate is tightly coupled to the last known data point, with very little room for structural change. At one week and one month, I incorporated the possibility of gradual drift while keeping the central tendency close to the recent level, reflecting a view that no major catalyst was imminent but that small positive momentum was marginally more likely than negative.

The confidence interval widths widen substantially as the horizon extends, which is the expected behavior for a time series forecast under uncertainty. At one day, the 90% interval spans only 3 units (37.8–40.8), reflecting that short-term volatility is relatively constrained and that most of the near-term distribution is tightly centered on the current value. By one week, the interval opens to 6 units, accounting for the accumulation of daily shocks. At one month, the interval reaches 14 units (33.0–47.0), reflecting meaningful uncertainty about the path over roughly 20 trading or calendar days. The widening follows roughly a square-root-of-time scaling, suggesting I was modeling something close to a random walk with modest drift.

Several specific risks could push outcomes outside these stated intervals. On the downside, an unexpected macroeconomic shock, a policy surprise, or a sharp deterioration in sentiment could drive values below 33 within a month. On the upside, a strong positive catalyst — an earnings surprise, a policy announcement, or a broad risk-on move — could push values well above 47. Liquidity events or structural breaks in the underlying data-generating process are particularly dangerous for interval forecasts because they invalidate the stationarity assumptions embedded in the width calculations.

It is also worth acknowledging that the one-month interval in particular may be underconfident or overconfident depending on the nature of the variable being forecast. If this is a financial price with fat-tailed returns, the true 90% interval is likely wider than stated. If it is a smoother macro or sentiment series, the interval may be appropriately sized. Regime changes — such as a shift from low-volatility to high-volatility environments — could cause all three intervals to be systematically too narrow, which is the most common failure mode for forecasts of this type.
